rulings.Case Title: CORAL ROPER ET AL VS. GENUINE PARTS COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR WITHDRAWAL OF ATTORNEY OF RECORD
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 1. PLAINTIFF CORAL ROPER , AS SUCCESSOR-IN-INTEREST TO AND AS WRONGFUL DEATH HEIR OF JAMES WILLIAMS, DECEASED MOTION FOR WITHDRAWAL OF ATTORNEY OF RECORD Nancy Williams, Brayton Purcell LLP, filed a Motion to be Relieved as Counsel for Plaintiff Coral Roper. No opposition filed. A hearing is required, and Counsel may appear in person or via Zoom. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.  Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest.  If a hearing is requested, it will be on December 17, 2024, at 9:00 a.m.  Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029.  Face coverings are optional.  The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSRs transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record.  If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost.  = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ALLETA BONNER ET AL VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 2. DEFENDANT P.E. O'HAIR & CO. ERRONEOUSLY SUED AS WESTBURNE SUPPLY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant P.E. O'Hair & Co's, Erroneously Sued as Westburne Supply, Inc's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Plaintiffs' evidentiary objections to Defendant's Exhibits E, F, G, H, and I in the Sandoval Declaration (relevant portions of Deposition Transcripts of Jospeh Fanelli and Robert Courtnier) are sustained. Defendant failed to satisfy either the unavailability or interest-and-motive requirement of Evidence Code section 1292, subdivision (a). (See Berroteran v. Superior Court (2022) 12 Cal.5th 867.) Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855; Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 [Under the standard enunciated in Aguilar, the defendant must make an affirmative showing that the plaintiff will be unable to prove his case by any means (cleaned up)].) As Plaintiffs note in their opposition, Defendant propounded special discovery on Plaintiffs, but failed to address the full scope of Plaintiffs' evidence or demonstrate that it was factually devoid in its entirety. Rather than comprehensibly addressing all identified sources of evidence, Defendant's motion selectively focuses on the prior testimony of two of Decedent's coworkers. Moreover, Defendant failed to depose the product identification witnesses disclosed in discovery. Plaintiffs identified themselves and Jospeh Fanelli and Robert Courtnier as persons with knowledge regarding Defendant's actions at Decedent's jobsites. (Sandoval Decl., Ex. D [Plaintiffs Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set one] at 6:12-15.) Defendant offers no evidence that it deposed or attempted to depose Decedent's coworkers. In the absence of evidence that Mr. Fanelli and Mr. Courtnier will be unable to testify that they recall working in Decedent's presence with asbestos-containing products sold or supplied by Defendant, the court cannot find that Plaintiffs will be unable to establish asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [stating that [c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered].) The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on December 17, 2024, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT P. SHULTZ VS. AK STEEL CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preference Pursuant To C.C.P. 36(A) And 36(D)
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 4(Part 1 of 2). PLAINTIFF ROBERT SHULTZ Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preference Pursuant To C.C.P. 36(A) And 36(D) Plaintiffs Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under C.C.P. 36(a). Oppositions filed. Joinders filed. Conditional non-opposition filed. Reply filed. Defendants oppositions to the Motion on due process grounds are meritless. Where a party meets the requisite standard for calendar preference under [Code of Civil Procedure section 36] subdivision (a), preference must be granted. No weighing of interests is involved.  (Fox v. Superior Court (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 529, 535; see also, e.g., Miller v. Superior Court (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 1200, 1204 [statute grants a mandatory and absolute right to trial preference]; Swaithes v. Superior Court (1989) 212 Cal.App.3d 1082, 1085 [trial court has no power to balance the different interests of opposing litigants in applying the provision]; Koch-Ash v. Superior Court (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 689, 694 [section 36(a) must be deemed to be mandatory and absolute and no discretion is left to trial courts.] 1. The trial date is April 14, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is April 16, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2.The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is April 8, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 5. The fact discovery cut-off date is March 28, 2025. 6. The expert discovery cut-off date is April 11, 2025. 7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than January 16, 2025. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. (Continued on the next line.)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA INGELS ET AL VS. BWDAC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Strike And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Prayer For Punitive Damages
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 5. (Motion 1 of 2). DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. Notice Of Motion To Strike And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Prayer For Punitive Damages Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetics Inc.s Motion to Strike Plaintiff Prayer for Punitive Damages is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendants request for judicial notice, Exhibit 1, is granted. (C.C.P. 451, 452, 453.) To state a claim for punitive damages, plaintiff must allege specific facts showing defendant has been guilty of malice, oppression or fraud. (Smith v. Superior Court (1992) 10 Cal. App. 4th 1033, 1042.) A motion to strike may lie where the facts alleged, if proven, would not support a finding that the defendant acted with malice, fraud or oppression. (Turman v. Turning Point of Central California (2010) 191 Cal. App. 4th 53, 63.) Plaintiffs have pled sufficient facts to support a claim of punitive damages under C.C.P. section 3294(a.) Plaintiffs allege that Decedent Lita A. Lawrence was exposed to asbestos from Defendants supplied asbestos-containing talc products. Plaintiffs have alleged that Defendants (including Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetics Inc.) manufactured, supplied, and sold asbestos-containing products with knowledge of high risk of injury and death resulting from exposure to asbestos products, knew that members of the general public such as Decedent would be exposed to asbestos and knew that individuals would assume that the asbestos products were safe, failed to provide a warning, and continued to market and sell products with knowledge of harm to human health. (Complaint at ¶¶ 32, 35-37, 39-42, 82-83.) These allegations, if proved, could support an award of punitive damages. (See SKF Farms v. Superior Court (1984) 153 Cal.App.3d 902, 906-907; see also Bankhead v. ArvinMeritor, Inc. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 68, 85 [intentionally marketing a defective product knowing that it might cause injury and death is highly reprehensible. (cleaned up); Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1300-1302.) (Continued on the next line.)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA INGELS ET AL VS. BWDAC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 5.(Part 1 of 3 for Motion 2 of 2). DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER to COMPLAINT Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetics Inc., Demurrer to Plaintiffs Complaint is OVVERULED in part and SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND in part. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendants request for judicial notice, Exhibit 1, is granted. (C.C.P. 451, 452, 453.) A demurrer tests the legal sufficiency of the complaint. (C.C.P 430.10(e); Rakestraw v. California Physicians Serv. (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 39, 42-43.) To withstand a demurrer, a plaintiff must show that the complaint alleges facts sufficient to establish every element of each cause of action. (Id. at p. 43.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) First Cause of Action The demurrer to the First Cause of Action for Negligence on the ground of failure to state a cause of action is overruled. Defendant argues that Plaintiffs Complaint does not identify any facts to support Plaintiffs claim that Defendant was negligent. The Court disagrees. In order to establish liability on a negligence theory, a plaintiff must prove duty, breach, causation, and damages. (Conroy v. Regents of University of California (2009) 45 Cal.4th 1244, 1250; Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal. 4th 1200, 1205.) Plaintiffs allege that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from talcum power products and/or talc that was used in the manufacture of those products. (See Exhibit A to Plaintiffs Complaint.) Plaintiffs allege that Defendant had a duty to exercise due care in pursuance of its activities (the supply of asbestos-containing products) and breached that duty of care by negligently and carelessly placing those products into the stream of commerce and failed to warn of known dangers. (Complaint, ¶¶ 22-36.) Plaintiffs further allege that as a proximate result of this negligence, Decedent was exposed to respirable asbestos and suffered permanent injuries, including mesothelioma. (Id. ¶¶ 17-18.) Contrary to Defendants argument, Plaintiffs are not required to plead specific facts affording an inference that Defendants conduct resulted in Decedents injuries. Additional factual details may be obtained through discovery. Accordingly, Defendants demurrer to the First Cause of Action is overruled. Second Cause of Action The demurrer to the Second Cause of Action for Strict Liability on the ground of failure to state a cause of action is overruled. Defendant argues Plaintiffs Complaint fails to identify any specific product used by the Decedent. The Court disagrees. Plaintiffs are not required to allege the specific product or products for which Defendant is responsible. The ruling in Bockrath v. Aldrich Chem Co. (1990) 21 Cal. 4th 71, 80, applies only when the pleaded facts of negligence and injury do not naturally give rise to an inference of causation, such as in a case involving numerous defendants who are sued for products liability based on long-term exposure to multiple toxins. (See Bockrath, 21 Cal.4th at 77 [Court granted review to decide how a complaint alleging harmful long-term exposure to multiple toxins must plead causation.].) In this case, Plaintiffs allege that Decedent was exposed to a single toxinasbestosand that Defendants products contained asbestos to which Decedent was exposed. (Complaint at ¶¶ 1, 5-8, 11, 15-17.) (Continued on the next line. Part 2 of 3; Motion 2 of 2))

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA INGELS ET AL VS. BWDAC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. NOTICE OF MOTION TO STRIKE AND MOTION TO STRIKE PLAINTIFFS PRAYER FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 5. (Part 2 of 2 for Motion 1 of 2) continued from previous line. DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. Notice Of Motion To Strike And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Prayer For Punitive Damages Defendant cites Morgan v. J-M Manufacturing Co., Inc. (2021) 60 Cal.App.5th 1078 to support its Motion. In that case, the court reversed an award of punitive damages following a jury verdict because there was no substantial evidence in the record that J-MM's officers, directors, and managing agents acted with the requisite state of mind to support an award of punitive damages. (Id. at p. 1091.) At the demurrer stage, however, the Courts inquiry is limited to the sufficiency of the allegations in the Complaint. At this stage, the Court cannot determine as a matter of law that those allegations are insufficient to support Plaintiffs prayer for punitive damages. Whether an officer, director or managing agent of Defendant was involved in the conduct of which Plaintiffs complain can be determined during discovery. Accordingly, Defendants motion to strike is denied. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.  Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest.  If a hearing is requested, it will be on December 17, 2024, at 9:00 a.m.  Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029.  Face coverings are optional.  The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSRs transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record.  If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost.  = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA INGELS ET AL VS. BWDAC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 5. (Part 2 of 3 for Motion 2 of 2). DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (Continued from previous line.) Moreover, the Complaint alleges with specificity that Decedents exposure to asbestos was due to her habitual use of various asbestos-containing cosmetic and personal hygiene products, and due to her close contact with her ex-husband who performed routine automotive repair work on friction material. (See Exhibit A to Plaintiffs Complaint.) The nature of Plaintiffs claim against Defendant is clear. (See Bockrath, 21 Cal.4th at 78 [In the ordinary personal injury lawsuit, in which the complaints factual recitations show plainly the connection between cause and effect, it suffices to plead causation succinctly and generally.]; see also Jones v. ConocoPhillips Co. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1187, 1194-1197 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrers without leave to amend where allegations of causation were sufficient under Bockrath].) As discussed above, there is no requirement that claims for negligence and strict products liability be alleged with particularity, and Plaintiffs are not required to include evidentiary details in the Complaint. Accordingly, Defendants demurrer to the Second Cause of Action is overruled. Third Cause of Action The demurrer to the Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation on the ground of failure to plead with sufficient specificity or particularly is sustained without leave to amend. The Court agrees with Defendant that Plaintiffs fail to specifically plead facts that meet the heightened pleading requirements for the Third Cause of Action. In addition, the Court notes Plaintiffs Opposition does not address the Negligent Misrepresentation Cause of Action. In an action for negligent misrepresentation, a plaintiff is required to show the same elements as intentional fraud, i.e., (1) false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure; (2) knowledge of falsity; (3) intent to defraud; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) damage, except they need not show elements two or three, i.e., scienter or intent to defraud. (Engalla v. Permanente Medical Group, Inc. (1997) 15 Cal.4th 951, 974; Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 174.) The facts that constitute the fraud must be alleged fully, factually, and specifically. (Wilhelm v. Pray, Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1324, 1331.) The particularity requirement necessitates the pleading facts that show how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representation was tendered, and pleading specifically the detriment proximately caused by the defendant's conduct. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 73; Service of Medallion, Inc. v. Clorox Co. (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1807, 1818.) When alleging fraud against a corporation, the plaintiff must allege the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representation, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written, unless by the allegations the defendant necessarily possesses full information on the facts or when the facts lie more in the knowledge of the defendant. (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 645; Tarmann v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. (1991) 2 Cal.App.4th 153, 157.) Here, Plaintiffs fail to plead fraud with any specificity against Defendant. The Complaint fails to set forth any facts as to alleged misrepresentations made by Defendant to Decedent, the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representations, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written. (Continued on the next line. Part 3 of 3; Motion 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA INGELS ET AL VS. BWDAC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2024, in Department 304, Line 5(Continued from previous line. Part 3 of 3; Motion 2 of 2). DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Instead, Plaintiffs attribute the purported misrepresentations to all co-defendants as a group, which is insufficient to meet the heightened pleading standards for fraud against a corporate defendant. Moreover, the Third Cause of Action is fatally defective. Plaintiffs Opposition does not suggest any amendment that could reasonably cure the fatal defects in the Complaint. Accordingly, the demurrer to Third Cause of Action is sustained without leave to amend. Fourth Cause of Action The demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure on the ground of failure to plead with sufficient specificity or particularly is overruled. Although Plaintiffs have not alleged misrepresentations by Defendant, they can base a cause of action for fraud by nondisclosure. To establish fraud through nondisclosure or concealment of facts, it is necessary to show the defendant was under a legal duty to disclose them. (OCM Principal Opportunities Fund v. CIBC World Markets Corp. (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 835, 845.) Nondisclosure or concealment may constitute actionable fraud when: (1) there is a fiduciary relationship between the parties; (2) the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff; (3) the defendant actively conceals a material fact from the plaintiff; and (4) the defendant makes partial representations but also suppresses some material facts. (Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc. (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 310-311.) However, a fiduciary relationship need not exist between the parties when the parties are in a relationship that gives rise to the duty, such as seller and buyer. (Id. at 311.) Such a relationship may arise from a transaction between the parties that involves direct dealings between the plaintiff and the defendant. (Id. at 311-312, citing authorities.) Defendant argues that Plaintiffs have not alleged a single fact establishing a fiduciary or confidential relationship between Plaintiffs and Defendant. However, as discussed above, such a relationship is not required. Allegations that the defendant was involved in retail sales of the disputed product to consumers are sufficient to establish a sufficient transaction or relationship between the parties. (Bader v. Johnson & Johnson (2022) 86 Cal.App.5th 1094, 1130-1132; see also Jones, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1198-1200 [complaint adequately alleged cause of action for fraudulent concealment where plaintiffs alleged that defendant manufacturers were aware of the toxic nature of their products and owed a duty to disclose those properties].) Here, the Complaint alleges that Defendant marketed, manufactured, sold, and supplied asbestos-containing products. (Complaint at ¶ 23.) Plaintiffs further allege that Defendant sold its products directly to Decedent, thereby creating a direct transactional relationship between Defendant and Decedent giving rise to a duty to disclose. (Id. at ¶ 69). Although sparse, nothing more is required at this stage of the litigation. (Jones, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1200.) Accordingly, Defendants demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action is overruled. = (304/EPS)
